text: Danger Signs in Pregnancy: CHW Job Aid

A danger sign is a warning sign that something is seriously wrong. When a pregnant woman has a danger sign, she needs to go to the health centre RIGHT AWAY. Do not wait to see if it gets better. Do not give home treatment first. Go to the health centre NOW.
What Are Danger Signs?

There are 8 main danger signs in pregnancy. Learn all of them. Every pregnant woman in your community should know these signs too. Teach them at every home visit. Put this card where the woman can see it.
Danger Sign 1: Bleeding from the Vagina

Any bleeding from the vagina during pregnancy is a danger sign. It does not matter how much — even a little blood is a warning. Bleeding can happen early in pregnancy (before 6 months) or late (after 6 months). Both are serious. Do not wait. Go to the health centre now.
⚠️ REFER NOW. Tell the health worker: 'She has bleeding from the vagina.' Bring any cloth or pad she used so the health worker can see how much she bled.
Danger Sign 2: Severe Headache or Blurred Vision

A bad headache that will not go away, or seeing spots or blurry things before the eyes, can be a sign of high blood pressure in pregnancy. This is very dangerous and can cause the woman to have a fit. She needs medicine RIGHT AWAY at the health centre.
⚠️ REFER NOW. Tell the health worker: 'She has a very bad headache and her vision is blurry.' Do not give aspirin or other pain medicine at home — go to the health centre.
Danger Sign 3: Fits or Seizures (Shaking of the Body)

If a pregnant woman suddenly shakes all over, loses control of her body, or loses consciousness (goes unconscious), this is a seizure. This is a medical emergency. It can happen because of very high blood pressure (this is called eclampsia). She could die without urgent treatment.

⚠️ REFER IMMEDIATELY — THIS IS AN EMERGENCY. Stay with her. Turn her gently on her side so she does not choke. Do not put anything in her mouth. Get help from everyone you can and go to the health centre right away.
Danger Sign 4: Fever

Fever during pregnancy (feeling very hot, temperature above 38°C or 100.4°F) can mean infection. Infection in pregnancy can harm both the mother and the baby. If she also has lower belly pain, smelly discharge, or just feels very unwell, the infection may be serious.
⚠️ REFER NOW. Tell the health worker: 'She has a fever and is pregnant.' Do not wait to see if the fever comes down.
Danger Sign 5: Very Bad Swelling of the Face, Hands, or Feet

Some swelling in the feet and legs in late pregnancy is normal. But if her face swells up, or if her hands are very puffy and she cannot make a fist, or if the swelling comes on suddenly, this is a danger sign. It can mean high blood pressure that is dangerous.
⚠️ REFER NOW. Tell the health worker: 'Her face and hands are very swollen.' Check if she also has headache or vision problems — these together are more urgent.
Danger Sign 6: Baby Is Not Moving

After 6 months of pregnancy, a woman should feel her baby move every day. If she has not felt the baby move all day, or the baby is moving much less than usual, this needs to be checked at the health centre. Sometimes the baby needs help.
⚠️ REFER TODAY. Tell the health worker: 'She cannot feel her baby moving.' The health worker will listen to the baby's heartbeat and check if everything is okay.
Danger Sign 7: Water Breaks Too Early

If water (clear or yellowish fluid) comes out from the vagina before the baby is due — especially before 8 months — this can mean the bag around the baby has broken early. This is serious because the baby can get infected. She needs to go to the health centre now, even if she has no pain.
⚠️ REFER NOW. Tell the health worker: 'Her water has broken and the baby is not due yet.' She should lie down during transport if possible.
Danger Sign 8: Difficulty Breathing
If a pregnant woman is breathing very fast, feels she cannot catch her breath, or has chest pain, go to the health centre right away. This can be a sign of a serious heart or lung problem.
⚠️ REFER NOW. Tell the health worker: 'She is having trouble breathing and she is pregnant.'
How to Help a Woman Get to the Health Centre

Every pregnant woman should have a birth plan that includes: who will take her to the health centre, how she will get there (vehicle, motorbike, being carried), what money is saved for transport, who will care for other children, and what to bring (health card, identity document, any medicines she takes).
Make this plan before the baby comes. If there is a danger sign, you will not have time to plan. Have the plan ready now. Talk to the family so everyone knows what to do.
What to Tell the Health Worker

When you arrive at the health centre, say clearly: (1) Her name, (2) How many months pregnant she is, (3) Which danger sign she has, (4) When the danger sign started, (5) Any other symptoms (fever, pain, bleeding amount). Bring her health card if she has one.
